name: Juicero
summary: Made a $400+ wifi-connected juicer that pressed proprietary produce packs; became a punchline when reporters showed the packs could be squeezed by hand just as fast.
lesson: Don't over-engineer a solution consumers can replicate with their bare hands.

name: Pebble
summary: Pioneering smartwatch that ran two record-breaking Kickstarter campaigns but couldn't compete with Apple Watch and Fitbit; assets acquired by Fitbit in 2016.
lesson: Crowdfunding hype doesn't guarantee a sustainable business against deep-pocketed competitors.

name: Lily Robotics
summary: Promised a throw-and-shoot autonomous camera drone using promo footage shot with a different prototype; shut down amid an SEC/DOJ fraud investigation before ever shipping.
lesson: Faking demo footage to sell preorders is fraud, and it eventually catches up with founders.

name: Essential Products
summary: Android co-founder Andy Rubin's company shipped the Essential Phone but sold poorly against Apple/Samsung; shut down in 2020.
lesson: Star founders and huge funding rounds can't overcome a saturated market and a single failed hero product.

name: Magic Leap
summary: Raised the largest AR war chest ever for its Magic Leap One headset, then missed consumer sales targets by orders of magnitude and pivoted entirely to enterprise/defense.
lesson: Enormous funding and secrecy can't substitute for validating real consumer demand before shipping.

name: Revolv
summary: Made a popular smart-home hub, was acquired by Google/Nest in 2014, then Nest bricked every customer's device in 2016 by shutting down the servers with no refunds.
lesson: Acquisition can be a death sentence for a hardware product if the acquirer has no roadmap for it.

name: Quirky
summary: Crowdsourced invention platform that launched Wink smart-home devices; overexpanded into too many simultaneous product lines and filed for bankruptcy in 2015.
lesson: Spreading capital across dozens of unproven products at once starves any one of them of the resources to succeed.

name: Bluesmart
summary: Made GPS-enabled smart luggage with built-in batteries and locks, then collapsed in 2018 after major airlines banned checked bags with non-removable batteries, wiping out its core product.
lesson: A hardware business built on airline cooperation is one policy change away from being wiped out.

name: 3D Robotics (3DR)
summary: Once a leading US consumer drone maker, it couldn't match DJI's pace of innovation and pricing, and pivoted entirely out of consumer hardware into enterprise drone software in 2016.
lesson: Competing against a vertically integrated, lower-cost overseas manufacturer on price is nearly impossible in consumer hardware.

name: Amazon Fire Phone
summary: Amazon's foray into smartphones with a 3D display flopped commercially, forcing a $170M inventory write-down and discontinuation within a year.
lesson: Gimmicky hardware features don't substitute for a compelling reason to switch platforms.

name: Project Ara (Google)
summary: Google's modular smartphone project let users swap out camera, battery, and other modules, but was cancelled in 2016 after failing to solve reliability and cost engineering challenges.
lesson: Modular hardware sounds compelling but real-world engineering tradeoffs can defeat the concept.

name: Basis Science
summary: Made an early fitness/sleep tracking smartwatch, was acquired by Intel in 2014, then Intel recalled and permanently shut down the product in 2016 over an overheating hazard.
lesson: A safety recall inside a corporate parent with no long-term wearables strategy usually ends in the product's death, not a fix.

name: Lytro
summary: Pioneered light-field cameras that let users refocus photos after the shot, but consumers didn't value the feature enough to justify the price; assets acquired by Google in 2018.
lesson: A technically brilliant camera innovation can still fail if it doesn't solve a problem consumers actually have.

name: Humane
summary: Ex-Apple designers built the AI Pin, a screenless wearable AI assistant that launched to scathing reviews; the company sold its assets to HP in 2025.
lesson: Star pedigree and hype cycles don't protect a product from fundamentally unsolved hardware and UX problems at launch.

name: Insteon
summary: One of the longest-running smart-home platforms, it abruptly shut down all servers and operations in April 2022 with no warning, bricking devices for thousands of customers.
lesson: Even a mature, long-established smart-home brand can vanish overnight if it runs out of cash.

name: Misfit
summary: Made stylish fitness trackers and was acquired by Fossil Group in 2015, but Fossil shut down the Misfit wearables brand in 2018.
lesson: A successful design-led exit doesn't guarantee the product survives inside a larger portfolio company.

name: Coin
summary: Made a single card that could store and emulate multiple credit/debit cards, was acquired by Fitbit in 2016 for its payments tech, then Fitbit discontinued it in 2017.
lesson: Payment-replacing hardware faces card-network and bank pushback that can undermine the product even after a successful launch.

name: Sonos
summary: Pioneered multi-room wireless speakers and went public on the NYSE in 2018, building a durable premium audio hardware and software ecosystem.
lesson: Owning both hardware and a proprietary multi-room software platform created lock-in that pure speaker makers couldn't match.

name: Withings
summary: French connected-health pioneer was bought by Nokia in 2016, then its founder bought the brand back in 2018 and continued growing it profitably.
lesson: A founder willing to buy back a struggling corporate-owned brand can rescue a strong product from bureaucratic neglect.

name: Garmin
summary: Originally a GPS navigation company that successfully expanded into running, cycling, diving and smartwatches, building one of the most durable sports-wearable franchises.
lesson: Deep vertical expertise let an incumbent out-execute newer wearable entrants in specialized athlete niches.

name: Whoop
summary: A screenless subscription fitness/recovery wearable favored by professional athletes, reached unicorn status by selling the band cheaply and monetizing via required membership fees.
lesson: Bundling hardware into a mandatory subscription can build a much larger recurring-revenue business than one-time hardware sales.

name: Beats by Dre
summary: Built a premium headphone brand around celebrity co-founders and pop-culture marketing, becoming Apple's largest acquisition at the time.
lesson: Brand and cultural marketing can differentiate consumer audio hardware even when the underlying components aren't class-leading.

name: Ecobee
summary: A Canadian smart-thermostat maker that competed directly with Nest, differentiating on remote-sensor room tracking, and was acquired by Generac in 2021.
lesson: A well-differentiated feature let a smaller player carve out a durable position next to a category-defining incumbent.

name: SmartThings
summary: An early Kickstarter-funded smart-home hub and open platform that was acquired by Samsung in 2014 and became the backbone of Samsung's connected-home strategy.
lesson: An open, developer-friendly smart-home platform can become strategically valuable enough for a hardware giant to acquire outright.

name: Wyze Labs
summary: Started as a spinout from Amazon employees and became known for radically undercutting smart camera and sensor prices while remaining profitable through direct sales.
lesson: Aggressively low pricing on smart-home hardware, paired with lean direct sales, can outcompete better-funded rivals on volume.

name: SimpliSafe
summary: Built a DIY, no-contract home security hardware and monitoring business that scaled into a multi-billion-dollar company, competing successfully against ADT.
lesson: Removing installation contracts and up-front costs can unlock a mass consumer market long dominated by expensive incumbents.

name: Anker
summary: Started selling replacement laptop batteries on Amazon, then expanded into chargers, power banks, and a wide consumer electronics portfolio.
lesson: Starting narrow on a marketplace with a commodity category and reinvesting profits can build a global consumer electronics conglomerate without heavy VC funding.

name: GoPro
summary: Created the action-camera category from a surfing wrist-strap camera idea and built a billion-dollar public company.
lesson: Creating and owning a new hardware category can build a durable brand even if growth slows once smartphones catch up.

name: Oculus VR
summary: A record Kickstarter campaign for the Rift VR headset led to a $2B acquisition by Facebook in 2014, and Oculus became the leading consumer VR hardware platform.
lesson: A crowdfunded prototype that nails timing on an emerging category can attract industry-altering acquisition interest within two years.

name: Skydio
summary: Built autonomous obstacle-avoiding drones using computer vision, becoming the leading US-based consumer/prosumer drone maker after most American rivals folded against DJI.
lesson: Winning against a dominant low-cost competitor requires genuinely differentiated technology, not just incremental improvements.

name: Roku
summary: Built the first mainstream streaming media player hardware, later expanding into its own smart TV operating system and ad business.
lesson: Hardware can be a loss-leader on-ramp to a much larger recurring software/advertising business.
